input.prompt:
I'm migrating my MSTest projects from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
My repo has a single Directory.Build.props at the root that is imported by
both production projects (e.g. `MyApp.csproj`) and test projects (e.g.
`MyApp.Tests.csproj`). I want to set `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` and the
MTP runner properties centrally, but I obviously don't want to turn my
library projects into executables. How do I write the condition in
Directory.Build.props so it only applies to the test projects?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm migrating my MSTest projects from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
My repo has a single Directory.Build.props at the root that is imported by
both production projects (e.g. `MyApp.csproj`) and test projects (e.g.
`MyApp.Tests.csproj`). I want to set `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` and the
MTP runner properties centrally, but I obviously don't want to turn my
library projects into executables. How do I write the condition in
Directory.Build.props so it only applies to the test projects?

data.content:
Use a naming-convention condition on `$(MSBuildProjectName)`:

```xml
<!-- Directory.Build.props -->
<Project>
  <!-- shared properties for all projects … -->

  <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

**Why not `$(IsTestProject)`?** — That property is set by `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` via NuGet, which is imported *after* `Directory.Build.props` runs, so it won't be available yet. A project-name convention (`.Tests`, `.UnitTests`, etc.) is evaluated immediately and is the most reliable approach.

If your test projects don't follow a single suffix, broaden the condition:

```xml
<PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.Contains('.Tests'))">
```

Or, if you prefer an opt-in flag, have each test `.csproj` set a property **above** its SDK import and check that instead:

```xml
<!-- MyApp.Tests.csproj -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <Import Project="Sdk.props" Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk" />
  ...
</Project>
```

But the name-convention approach keeps things centralized with zero per-project boilerplate.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about how to conditionally set MSBuild properties in a `Directory.Build.props` file so they only apply to test projects (not production/library projects) when migrating from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP).

This is a knowledge question — no codebase exploration needed.

The standard approach is to use an MSBuild condition that checks for the presence of the MSTest runner package reference, or more practically, to condition on a naming convention or on whether a test-related package is referenced.

The cleanest approaches:

1. **Condition on project name convention** — e.g., `Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))"` or similar.


2. **Have test projects explicitly set a property** — test projects define something like `<IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>`, then the shared props file conditions on that.
3. **Condition on `IsTestProject`** — The `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` package sets `$(IsTestProject)` automatically, but there's a timing issue since SDK imports happen after `Directory.Build.props` evaluation, so this approach might not work reliably in the shared props file.

The real issue is that `IsTestProject` gets set by NuGet imports, which come after `Directory.Build.props` but before `Directory.Build.targets`. So I could use `Directory.Build.targets` where `IsTestProject` is available, or stick with `Directory.Build.props` using a naming convention like checking if the project name ends with `.Tests`. For setting `OutputType` and runner properties, the naming convention approach in `Directory.Build.props` is actually the simplest and most reliable.
